# Why Is Global Cooperation Essential for Climate Action?

![A striking mirrored scene symbolizes the effects of climate change with a towering iceberg and craggy rocks highlighting arctic vulnerability. Environmental degradation related to rising global temperatures causing glacial melt is prevalent. This necessitates the need for global climate action fostering renewable energy development.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/climate-change-reflection-a-symmetrical-view-of-environmental-crisis-and-ice.jpg "Climate Change Reflection a Symmetrical View of Environmental Crisis and Ice Melt Sustainability Concerns")

## Fundamentals

Climate change stands as a formidable global challenge, demanding immediate and comprehensive action. Its impacts, ranging from rising sea levels and extreme weather events to disruptions in ecosystems and food security, are not confined by national borders. Addressing this crisis effectively necessitates a united front, a concept embodied in **global cooperation**. Without countries working together, the scale of the problem becomes insurmountable, and the consequences become increasingly dire for all nations, regardless of their individual contributions to the issue.

Global cooperation is the bedrock upon which effective climate action is built, essential for tackling a challenge that transcends national boundaries.

Consider the very nature of greenhouse gas emissions. Carbon dioxide and other pollutants released into the atmosphere disperse globally, contributing to a collective problem. No single nation, even the largest emitters, can unilaterally solve climate change. Actions taken by one country to reduce emissions can be undermined if others continue on a high-emission pathway.

This interconnectedness underscores the fundamental need for collaborative strategies and shared responsibility. Imagine trying to bail out a sinking boat where everyone is throwing water overboard → unless everyone starts bailing in sync, the boat will continue to take on water.

![A desiccated surface fractures in shades of beige and blue highlighting the damaging effects of climate change and desertification. This visual represents environmental degradation, ecosystem fragility, and the urgent need for conservation. The harsh textures symbolize resource depletion, calling for strategies toward a sustainable future including renewable energy adoption to address global warming and foster climate resilience.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/cracked-earth-surface-depicting-climate-change-and-ecological-stress.jpg "Cracked Earth Surface Depicting Climate Change and Ecological Stress Illustrating Environmental Sustainability Challenges")

### Why Act Together?

The reasons for [global cooperation Meaning → Global Cooperation, in the context of sustainability, denotes the imperative of unified action across nations, organizations, and individuals.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/area/global-cooperation/)  on climate action are multifaceted, stemming from both practical necessities and ethical considerations. Let’s explore some key aspects:

In essence, global cooperation is not merely a desirable option; it is an **imperative** for effective climate action. It is the mechanism through which humanity can pool its resources, share its knowledge, and coordinate its efforts to confront this unprecedented challenge and build a sustainable [future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  for all.

## Intermediate

Building upon the fundamental understanding of why global cooperation is essential, we now consider the complexities and nuances of achieving effective collaborative climate action. While the necessity of working together is widely acknowledged, the path to genuine and impactful cooperation is fraught with challenges. These hurdles range from diverging national interests and geopolitical tensions to disparities in economic capabilities and varying levels of climate vulnerability. Navigating these complexities requires a deeper examination of the mechanisms, obstacles, and potential pathways for successful global climate governance.

Effective global climate cooperation, while essential, faces significant hurdles stemming from diverse national interests and uneven capabilities, demanding sophisticated governance mechanisms.

## Academic

From an academic perspective, the imperative for global cooperation on [climate action Meaning → Climate Action: Deliberate steps to address climate change impacts through mitigation and adaptation strategies for a sustainable future.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/climate-action/)  transcends simple notions of shared responsibility or resource pooling. It is rooted in the very **systemic nature** of the Earth’s climate and the deeply interconnected web of human societies. Climate change, viewed through a systems lens, is not merely an environmental problem but a complex, multi-dimensional challenge that intersects with economics, politics, social justice, and ethical considerations.

Effective response, therefore, necessitates a paradigm shift towards collaborative [governance Meaning → Governance, in an ESG context, refers to the systems of rules, practices, and processes by which a company is directed and controlled.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  structures that acknowledge and address this intricate interconnectedness. This section will analyze the theoretical underpinnings of global climate cooperation, explore the limitations of current approaches, and propose avenues for more robust and transformative collaborative frameworks.

Academic analysis reveals global climate cooperation as a systemic necessity, demanding a shift towards integrated governance frameworks that address the intricate interconnectedness of climate change.

Light and color are used to enhance a global vision of net zero emissions, ecological integrity, biodiversity conservation, promoting collaborative efforts toward environmental protection and planetary health ultimately aiding climate resilience.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-sphere-of-future-earth-resources-embracing-ecological-balance-and.jpg "Sustainable Sphere of Future Earth Resources Embracing Ecological Balance and Mitigating Climate Change Risks")

### What Systemic Challenges Hinder Cooperation?

Academic research highlights several systemic challenges that inherently impede effective global climate cooperation. These challenges go beyond the previously discussed barriers and delve into deeper structural and conceptual issues:

| Systemic Challenge | Description | Impact on Cooperation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Tragedy of the Commons** | Climate change exemplifies a classic “tragedy of the commons” scenario. The atmosphere is a shared resource, and individual nations acting in their short-term self-interest can deplete this resource, leading to collective harm. | Undermines cooperation by incentivizing free-riding and hindering collective action towards emissions reduction. |
| **Collective Action Problems** | Climate action is a global public good, meaning that its benefits are non-excludable and non-rivalrous. This creates collective action problems, where individual actors may be reluctant to contribute to the public good if they believe others will free-ride or if their individual contribution seems insignificant. | Hampers the mobilization of sufficient collective effort and resources for effective climate mitigation and adaptation. |
| **Distributional Conflicts** | Climate change and climate policies have significant distributional consequences, both within and between countries. The costs and benefits of climate action are not evenly distributed, leading to conflicts over burden sharing and equity. | Creates political obstacles to cooperation as nations and groups within nations compete for resources and resist perceived unfair burdens. |
| **Temporal Mismatches** | The long-term nature of climate change and the short-term focus of political and economic systems create temporal mismatches. The benefits of climate action are often realized in the long term, while the costs may be incurred in the short term, leading to a bias towards inaction. | Reduces political incentives for ambitious climate policies and undermines sustained commitment to long-term climate goals. |
| **Epistemic Uncertainty and Complexity** | Climate science, while robust, still involves uncertainties and complexities. Understanding the precise impacts of climate change and the effectiveness of different mitigation and adaptation strategies remains an ongoing scientific endeavor. | Can create skepticism and delay action, particularly in the face of competing narratives and vested interests that exploit uncertainty. |

# The Power of International Cooperation in Solving Global Environmental Challenges

by [EcoServants®](https://ecoservantsproject.org/author/ecoservants/ "Posts by EcoServants®") | Feb 6, 2025 | [General Discussion](https://ecoservantsproject.org/category/general-discussion/) | [0 comments](https://ecoservantsproject.org/the-power-of-international-cooperation-in-solving-global-environmental-challenges/#respond)

At EcoServants, we deeply appreciate the hard work and dedication of our interns as they contribute to critical research and solutions for environmental sustainability. Today, we want to highlight the valuable insights of our data intern, Joudy Alkheder, whose research underscores the importance of international cooperation in addressing global environmental challenges.

### **Why International Cooperation Matters**

The challenges our planet faces—climate change, biodiversity loss, and pollution—transcend national borders. No single country can tackle these issues alone. Effective international cooperation is vital for exchanging knowledge, technologies, and strategies to mitigate the environmental crisis. Through global agreements and coordinated action, nations can make meaningful progress toward sustainability.

According to research published in the *Journal of International Politics* by Abu Al-Qasim (2020), international cooperation has been a crucial factor in addressing environmental concerns, particularly in the post-Cold War era. The study highlights that collective efforts between nations have significantly influenced global environmental policy and governance, shaping a more united front against ecological degradation.

### **Key Benefits of Global Environmental Cooperation**

1. **Sharing Knowledge and Technology**  
   Countries with advanced sustainability practices can help others by sharing green technology, research, and best practices. This collaboration fosters innovation and accelerates the adoption of eco-friendly solutions worldwide. For example, Germany’s investment in renewable energy has led to breakthroughs in solar panel efficiency, which have benefited developing nations seeking to transition to clean energy sources. Additionally, international organizations like the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) facilitate knowledge-sharing platforms that bring together experts to discuss the latest advancements in environmental sustainability.
2. **Strengthening International Agreements**  
   Multilateral agreements like the Montreal Protocol and the Convention on Biological Diversity provide frameworks for countries to set and meet environmental goals. The Montreal Protocol, for instance, has successfully led to a significant reduction in ozone-depleting substances, preventing millions of cases of skin cancer and reducing harmful UV radiation exposure worldwide. Similarly, the Kyoto Protocol, which laid the foundation for global carbon emission reduction, demonstrated how collective commitments can lead to tangible improvements in environmental health.
3. **Coordinated Response to Climate Change**  
   Extreme weather events, rising sea levels, and shifting ecosystems require global coordination to mitigate their effects. International cooperation ensures that nations support one another in disaster preparedness, resource allocation, and climate adaptation. According to the United Nations, climate-related disasters caused economic losses exceeding $2.5 trillion globally between 2000 and 2019, highlighting the urgent need for a unified response. Countries like the Netherlands, known for their expertise in flood management, have provided guidance to flood-prone regions like Bangladesh, demonstrating the benefits of shared experience and technology in climate adaptation strategies.
4. **Preserving Global Biodiversity**  
   Conservation efforts must be global because species migration and ecosystem shifts do not recognize political boundaries. Collaborative initiatives, such as cross-border wildlife corridors and international marine protected areas, help safeguard biodiversity. The Amazon Rainforest, spanning nine countries, serves as a crucial example of a region requiring multinational cooperation to prevent deforestation and protect thousands of species. The Great Green Wall project in Africa is another ambitious international effort, involving over 20 countries working together to combat desertification and restore degraded land across the Sahel region.
5. **Economic and Political Stability**  
   Environmental degradation can lead to conflicts over resources, displacement, and economic instability. Through cooperation, nations can promote sustainable resource management, reducing the risk of environmental-driven conflicts. For instance, the Nile Basin Initiative fosters collaboration between African nations that rely on the Nile River, preventing disputes over water resources and ensuring long-term sustainability. Similarly, efforts to manage shared resources like the Arctic, where melting ice opens new opportunities and risks, require diplomatic coordination to prevent geopolitical tensions while ensuring environmental protection.

### **A Call to Strengthen Global Partnerships**

Joudy Alkheder’s research reminds us that international cooperation is not just beneficial—it is essential. As climate change continues to impact ecosystems and economies worldwide, nations must prioritize partnerships to achieve a sustainable future. Through global collaboration, we can foster resilience, innovation, and shared responsibility in protecting our planet.

According to a 2021 report from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), limiting global warming to 1.5°C will require unprecedented international cooperation, particularly in emissions reductions, carbon capture technologies, and financial support for developing nations. The World Bank estimates that by 2050, over 143 million people could become climate migrants if proactive environmental policies are not implemented globally. These figures illustrate the pressing need for nations to act collectively in mitigating environmental crises.

At EcoServants, we are proud to support interns like Joudy, who bring forward research that informs and inspires action. By acknowledging the role of international cooperation, we can advocate for policies and practices that strengthen environmental sustainability on a global scale.

**Source:**  
Abu Al-Qasim, F. B. A. (2020). Environmental issues and their impact on international relations in the post-Cold War world. *Journal of International Politics.*

International Cooperation: Key to Combating the Climate Crisis
==============================================================

Ecology Policy

Level

![Image 13: article cover](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/ZvwrurVsGrYSwP-V_international-cooperation-key-to-combating-the-climate-crisis-3.jpg?auto=format%2Ccompress&w=896&h=256&fit=max&dpr=3)

![Image 14: Kara Anderson](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/f9859071-2178-4b79-8ed7-3269e73cdd6e_Image-1+%281%29.jpg?auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=max&w=3000&dpr=3)

This article explores why global collaboration is essential to combating climate change and how partnerships, shared technologies, and joint policies are making an impact.

Ecology

2024-09-25T00:00:00.000Z

2025-06-02T00:00:00.000Z

en-us

From devastating wildfires to unprecedented floods, the climate crisis is reshaping life on Earth as we know it. **But one thing is clear: [climate change](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/climate-change-in-2022-where-do-we-stand) doesn’t respect borders**. Whether it’s [rising sea levels](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/sea-level-rise-what-is-happening?utm_source=adwords&utm_medium=ppc&utm_campaign=1:%20SN%20|%202:%20Brand%20|%203:%20Search%20Brand%20google%20|%204:%20FR), prolonged droughts, or increasingly unpredictable storms, these challenges affect us all, and no nation can escape the consequences alone.

“ As we face this escalating global emergency, the need for international cooperation has never been more urgent. Isolated efforts may offer temporary relief, but only collective action can deliver long-term solutions. ”

**In this article, we'll explore why working together is the only viable solution to combating climate change. We'll uncover how global partnerships, shared technologies, and joint climate policies are already making an impact and why isolationist approaches fall short in addressing the scale of the crisis.**

Why international cooperation is essential in the climate crisis
----------------------------------------------------------------

“ Climate change is a global threat, and no nation can tackle it alone. From wildfires in Canada to floods in Pakistan, the impacts are being felt on every continent. That’s because climate change doesn’t respect borders. ”

### Shared causes, shared consequences

Greenhouse gas [emissions](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/carbon-emissions-what-you-need-to-know) produced in one country contribute to rising global temperatures everywhere. **The atmosphere, oceans, and [ecosystems](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/ecosystem-definition-components-and-structure) are all deeply interconnected**. Melting [glaciers](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/what-is-the-doomsday-glacier-and-why-does-it-matter) raise sea levels far from the poles. [Warmer oceans](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/why-are-ocean-temperatures-breaking-records) fuel storms thousands of miles away. No country is immune.

### Unequal impacts

Some nations contribute less to the crisis but face the harshest consequences. **Africa, for example, accounts for just [3.8%](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/africa-faces-an-uphill-battle-against-western-emissions-to-combat-climate-change) of global emissions, yet it’s among the hardest hit**, with increasing droughts, floods, and food insecurity. These countries need international support to adapt and build resilience.

### The power of global collaboration

Without cooperation and a common understanding of responsibilities and goals, efforts to tackle climate change will be fragmented and fall short. International agreements like the [Paris Agreement](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/paris-agreement-all-you-need-to-know) create a unifying framework that brings jurisdictions together to:

🌍

 Exchange information on emissions trends, best practices, and policy outcomes to improve decision-making globally 

📉

 Coordinate emissions reduction efforts to maximize global impact and avoid duplication 

🤝

 Support vulnerable regions with finance and technology, and provide assistance where it's needed most 

🌐

 Build trust and alignment between international partners to ensure coordinated and equitable action 

These collective efforts provide the best chance of limiting global warming, building resilience, and responding effectively to one of the most complex global challenges humanity has ever faced.

### A unified response

“ The scale of the climate crisis demands a unified global response. Through partnerships that pool resources, share technologies, and create coordinated policies, we can begin to address this immense challenge. ”

Close

![Image 17: youtube screenshot](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/aD2iRLh8WN-LVeug_%5D.jpg?auto=format%2Ccompress&w=894&fit=max&dpr=3)

How effective is international cooperation?
-------------------------------------------

International cooperation is crucial in the fight against climate change, and when it works, it works remarkably well. But it’s not always straightforward. **Some agreements have delivered measurable results, while others have suffered from a lack of enforcement, ambition, or political will.**

Let's take a closer look at some of the key international frameworks created to facilitate international cooperation to tackle climate change:

A proven success: the Montreal Protocol and the ozone layer

**Background:** In the 1980s, scientists discovered that chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) were causing a hole in the ozone layer – a critical shield that protects life on Earth from harmful UV radiation.

**Action:** In 1987, countries came together under the Montreal Protocol to phase out the production and consumption of CFCs and other ozone-depleting substances.

**Results:**

*   Over 99% of ozone-depleting chemicals have been eliminated.
*   The ozone layer is on track to recover by the 2060s, according to UNEP and NASA.
*   The phaseout of these substances has achieved over 20 times the emissions reductions of the Kyoto Protocol’s first commitment period – and at a fraction of the cost.

This is often cited as the most successful multilateral environmental treaty ever signed, showing what’s possible when science, diplomacy, and political commitment align.

A global framework with mixed results: the Paris Agreement

**Adopted in 2015**, the Paris Agreement is the cornerstone of today’s international climate efforts. It marked a turning point:

*   **Widespread participation:** Nearly every country is party to the agreement.

**Key goals:**

*   Limit global warming to well below 2°C and ideally 1.5°C.
*   Require countries to submit and regularly update Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs).
*   Encourage technology sharing, climate finance, and capacity building.

**Effectiveness to date:**

*   Created an enduring global framework for cooperation.
*   Mobilized trillions in climate finance.
*   Introduced a “ratchet mechanism” to raise ambition over time.

**Challenges:**

*   Current pledges fall short – likely to result in 2.5–2.9°C of warming.
*   Implementation has been inconsistent.
*   No binding enforcement mechanisms – relies on peer pressure and transparency.

Other international mechanisms and institutions

#### Kyoto Protocol (1997)

*   First legally binding climate treaty for developed nations.
*   Created the Clean Development Mechanism (CDM).
*   Limited impact – many countries missed targets.

#### UNFCCC

*   Established in 1992.
*   Hosts COP climate conferences and supports ongoing coordination.
*   Helps align national systems with global climate goals.

#### IPCC

*   Scientific body – not policymaking.
*   Provides the evidence base for international climate action.

#### Green Climate Fund

*   Aims to channel $100 billion per year to developing nations.
*   Focus on adaptation and sustainable development.

#### Global Methane Pledge (2021)

*   Voluntary agreement to cut methane 30% by 2030.
*   Signed by over 150 countries.

### Is international cooperation effective?

**When international cooperation is backed by strong political will, clear goals, and financial support, it works.** The Montreal Protocol is proof of that.

**But with climate change, the stakes are higher, the timeline is tighter, and the solutions are more complex**. While the Paris Agreement is a monumental diplomatic achievement, the real test is in delivery, and so far, global progress is uneven and too slow.

“ International cooperation is necessary, but not sufficient on its own. It provides the frameworks, mechanisms, and momentum for collective action. Without it, the fight against climate change would be fragmented, inequitable, and far less effective. With it, we have a fighting chance – if countries are willing to follow through. ”

Why isolationism doesn't work
-----------------------------

In the face of a global crisis like climate change, **isolationism doesn’t work**. In fact,**it’s not just ineffective, it’s actively counterproductive.**

### Climate change knows no borders

Some political movements push for a focus on national interests over global cooperation. But climate change isn’t confined to one country. Emissions from one region affect weather patterns, sea levels, and ecosystems across the planet.

**Isolationist policies ignore the interconnected nature of Earth’s climate systems and the deep economic and environmental links between nations.**

### No country can do it alone

Even the biggest emitters - China, the United States, and the European Union - can’t solve the crisis without working together. Fragmented efforts will always fall short of what’s needed.

And beyond these powers, **developing nations play a crucial role**. Many lack the resources to adapt or decarbonise on their own. **They need****technology transfers, climate finance, and shared expertise**, all of which only happen through international cooperation.

### Isolationism holds back innovation and growth

**Global collaboration isn’t just about emissions, it’s also about****economic opportunity**. Countries investing in clean technology and enforcing high regulatory standards are building new industries and creating jobs. When nations work together, they:

 Lower costs for new technologies like green hydrogen or electric vehicles 

 Speed up deployment by scaling production and sharing best practices 

 Open global markets for climate-friendly goods and services 

Isolationism slows all of this down and leaves countries less competitive in the green economy.

### Supply chains are global

**Most climate solutions depend on international supply chains**. A single solar panel might involve:

 Materials from Africa and Asia 

 Manufacturing in Europe 

 Technology developed in the US 

Restricting global cooperation makes these systems less efficient, driving up costs and delaying progress.

“ In short: isolationism weakens our response to climate change. It slows innovation, increases costs, deepens inequality, and makes the global transition to a low-carbon future harder to achieve. ”

Only through international cooperation can we unlock the scale, speed, and solidarity needed to safeguard the planet.

![Image 22: wind farm](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/01970593-f26a-462b-ba43-f72ecd9053d2_olympics+article+image2.jpg?auto=format%2Ccompress&w=896&fit=max&dpr=3)

Why sharing knowledge and technology is essential
-------------------------------------------------

“ One of the greatest strengths of international cooperation is the ability to share solutions across borders. No single country has all the answers to climate change, but by pooling technologies, resources, and insights, we can accelerate global progress. ”

### Collaboration drives faster innovation

When countries work together, they:

🔋

Share successful technologies like renewable energy systems, electric vehicles, and low-carbon materials

📘

Learn from one another’s experiences, avoiding repeated mistakes

🤝

Lower costs through joint development and scaled deployment

This kind of exchange doesn’t just speed things up, it also delivers better outcomes by making progress more equitable and efficient.

### The Breakthrough Agenda: a blueprint for action

Launched at COP26, the [Breakthrough Agenda](https://breakthroughagenda.org/) is a powerful example of how international collaboration can drive real-world results.

Led by the [IEA](https://www.iea.org/), [IRENA](https://www.irena.org/), and UN Climate [Champions](https://climatechampions.unfccc.int/un-climate-change-high-level-champions/) - in collaboration with a range of global partners - the initiative focuses on five key sectors responsible for over 60% of global emissions:

⚡

Power

🚗

Road transport

🏗️

Steel

🧪

Hydrogen

🌾

Agriculture

Its mission? **To make clean technologies in these sectors the most affordable, accessible, and attractive options worldwide by 2030.**

### Technology transfer: scaling solutions globally

One of the Breakthrough Agenda’s most valuable components is technology transfer, where leading nations share breakthroughs with others. This sharing reduces the duplication of efforts, lowers barriers to entry, and ensures that innovations and other forms of practical support can reach those who need them most.

### Knowledge matters as much as tech

While access to technology is critical, **knowledge sharing is equally vital,** especially for developing countries facing the harshest climate impacts.

These countries need expertise in:

🌾

Adaptation strategies, like drought-resistant agriculture or flood-proof infrastructure

⚠️

Early warning systems for extreme weather

🏙️

Climate-resilient urban planning

By sharing best practices, research, and policy models, international initiatives can boost resilience where it’s needed most. Civil society actors – including NGOs, local networks, and educational institutions – are often key players in this knowledge transfer, helping tailor global insights to local realities.

“ Technology and knowledge sharing are the twin pillars of international climate cooperation. Without these, the world would struggle to meet the scale and speed of the transformation required. ”

The Breakthrough Agenda helps bridge these gaps, not just by circulating ideas and innovations, but by building the policy and financial foundations that make global deployment possible.

International cooperation and climate finance
---------------------------------------------

**While sharing technology and knowledge is essential in the fight against climate change, financial cooperation is the foundation that makes action possible**.

“ Without adequate funding, even the best strategies can’t be implemented, especially in countries that are already suffering the most from climate impacts. ”

### A historic imbalance

**Countries in the Global North (including the United States and EU members) are responsible for around [79%](https://www.cgdev.org/media/who-caused-climate-change-historically#:~:text=Developed%20Countries%20Are%20Responsible%20for,Emissions%20%7C%20Center%20For%20Global%20Development) of global CO₂ emissions since the 19th century**. Meanwhile, Africa contributes just 3.8% of global emissions, yet faces some of the harshest climate consequences, including droughts, floods, and food insecurity.

The IPCC estimates that **individuals in developing countries are [15 times](https://www.undp.org/belarus/stories/loss-and-damage-fund-developing-countries#:~:text=According%20to%20the%20Intergovernmental%20Panel,natural%20disasters%20than%20developed%20countries.) more likely to be affected by climate-related disasters than those in wealthier nations.**

### Climate finance: what exists?

To address this imbalance, a range of international climate finance mechanisms has been created:

 💰 
Green Climate Fund (GCF)

Supports low-carbon and climate-resilient projects in developing countries. So far, it has mobilised over $10.3 billion.

 🌿 
Adaptation Fund (AF)

Finances projects that help communities adapt to changing climate conditions, from coastal protections to sustainable agriculture.

 🌊 
Loss and Damage Fund

Established at COP27, this fund helps vulnerable countries recover from irreversible climate impacts like sea-level rise, floods, and wildfires.

“ These mechanisms form part of a broader framework for climate finance, but in practice, they are severely underfunded. ”

### Broken promises

In 2009, developed nations pledged to provide [$100 billion](https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/sub-issues/climate-finance-and-the-usd-100-billion-goal.html)annually in climate finance to developing countries by 2020.

**The target was missed in 2020 and 2021**

**It was only met in 2022, years behind schedule**

**Trust has eroded, making climate negotiations at events like COP28 more difficult**

The [Loss and Damage Fund](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/company-guide/what-is-the-cop27-loss-and-damage-fund) faces a particularly stark gap.**While the estimated need is [$400](https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2023/dec/06/700m-pledged-to-loss-and-damage-fund-cop28-covers-less-than-02-percent-needed) billion per year, current pledges total just [$661](https://unfccc.int/process-and-meetings/bodies/funds-and-financial-entities/loss-and-damage-fund-joint-interim-secretariat/pledges-to-the-loss-and-damage-fund) million, barely a fraction of what’s required.**

### Private sector: the missing piece?

Public funds alone won’t be enough. Many hoped that international institutions like the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank could use public money to de-risk investments and attract private capital into developing countries.

But so far, progress has been limited:

**Trillions in private finance across the financial sector remain largely untapped**

**Regulatory and political risks in developing countries continue to deter investment**

**Projects often lack the bankability or scale to appeal to global capital markets**

### A double standard

What makes the situation even more difficult for developing nations is the contradiction in global finance priorities.

**In 2022 alone, the G20 provided over $1 trillion in fossil fuel subsidies**

**That’s more than ten times what developing countries have received for climate resilience**

This stark imbalance fuels frustration at climate summits, where vulnerable countries continue to demand greater accountability and equity from wealthier nations.

“ The scale of the climate crisis demands far more ambition, solidarity, and follow-through from developed nations. Without financial justice and the resources to act, the global response to climate change will remain deeply unequal and dangerously ineffective. ”

Close

![Image 23: ZvPIwLVsGrYSv7Bc Screenshot2024 09 25at10](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/ZvPIwLVsGrYSv7Bc_Screenshot2024-09-25at10.24.16.png?auto=format%2Ccompress&w=894&fit=max&dpr=3)

Where is international cooperation delivering results?
------------------------------------------------------

We've already explored the high-level frameworks, like the Paris Agreement and climate finance mechanisms, that lay the foundation for global climate action. But international cooperation isn’t limited to treaties and funding pledges.

In practice, **collaborative efforts across key sectors are already driving real-world change**. From decarbonizing heavy industry to scaling up electric vehicles and making agriculture more climate-resilient, these joint initiatives show how cooperation translates into measurable progress.

Decarbonizing heavy industry: progress on green steel

Steel production accounts for roughly 7–9% of global emissions, but recent international cooperation is starting to push the sector towards emissions reduction.

Through partnerships formed under the Breakthrough Agenda, countries have begun aligning standards for low-emission steel, helping manufacturers adopt greener processes. Shared research, investment coordination, and harmonised policy signals are helping to bring near-zero emission steel to market faster.

This is a critical development in one of the world’s hardest-to-abate sectors and a clear sign that collective action can unlock change at scale.

Scaling clean transport: electric vehicles on the rise

The electric vehicle (EV) market is another area where global cooperation is paying off.

In 2023, EVs made up 18% of global car sales, up from just 2% five years earlier. International coordination on charging infrastructure, regulations, and supply chains has helped reduce costs and improve availability.

Leading countries have also shared incentives and R&D to accelerate uptake.

The result? A faster shift away from fossil fuel vehicles and a more competitive global EV market.

Protecting food systems: climate-smart agriculture

Agriculture is both a victim and a contributor to climate change. But international partnerships and bilateral agreements are helping to protect food security while cutting emissions.

Initiatives like the Global Alliance for Climate-Smart Agriculture are:

*   Developing drought-tolerant and flood-resistant crops
*   Promoting sustainable land and water management practices
*   Sharing techniques to reduce methane and nitrous oxide emissions from farming

These efforts are especially vital in regions facing hunger, poverty, and worsening climate extremes, and they demonstrate how knowledge and resource sharing can improve resilience on a global scale.

Many countries in the Global South are also emerging as leaders in agricultural innovation. Nations like India, Kenya, and Brazil are pioneering approaches at the regional level in climate-resilient farming, agroforestry, and off-grid irrigation systems that offer valuable lessons for others, showing that solutions aren’t just flowing North to South, but also South to South.

“ From greener steel to smarter farming, international cooperation is turning ambition into action. These successes show that when countries align their goals and pool their efforts, they can drive change across entire sectors faster than any nation could achieve alone. ”

![Image 24: people in a field planting trees](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/fd3ce1fb-c570-4676-9856-383ff8a882ff_carbon+sink+article+image+2.jpg?auto=format%2Ccompress&w=896&fit=max&dpr=3)

What’s holding international cooperation back?
----------------------------------------------

Despite major frameworks and growing urgency, climate cooperation at the global level continues to fall short, not just because of ambition gaps, but because of structural and political barriers that make progress uneven and difficult to sustain.

Some of the biggest challenges include:

 Uneven national priorities

--- SOURCE 5: Global Climate Solutions Require Global Cooperation ---
URL: https://www.owu.edu/news-media/from-our-perspective/global-climate-solutions-require-global-cooperation/

SUMMARY:
The Paris Climate Agreement is not the end-all answer to the problem of climate change, but it's an important step in the right direction.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.owu.edu/images/print_logo.png)

# From Our Perspective

#### "We can put 'America First,' only if we first put America at the international negotiating table in good faith to talk with our global community about our many options for tackling climate change."

## Global Climate Solutions Require Global Cooperation

### By Laurie Anderson, Ph.D., and Nathan Rowley, Ph.D.

**![](https://www.owu.edu/files/resources/medium_laurel-anderson.jpg)****![](https://www.owu.edu/files/resources/small-nathan-rowley.jpg)****Our world has shrunk so much in this age of global markets and global climate change that it's time to update John Donne: No *nation* is an island unto itself.**  
Our planet is an integrated physical and biological system. When it comes to the global climate, the nations and people of the world are fully integrated.  
  
It is simply unreasonable for the United States of America to back away from the Paris Climate Agreement, not so much because of the specific details of the voluntary commitments made by 195 out of 198 of the world's nations, but because of its collaborative nature, and because it represents a truly global commitment to make progress as one people.  
  
The Paris Climate Agreement is not the end-all answer to the problem of climate change, but it's an important step in the right direction – and it represents the spirit of international cooperation that is essential to working toward a global solution. President Trump's decision to sit this one out brings peril to our planet, to the U.S. economy, and to our nation's reputation as a global citizen.

![](https://www.owu.edu/files/resources/medium_laurel-anderson.jpg)
![](https://www.owu.edu/files/resources/small-nathan-rowley.jpg)

Defining the problem of climate change has been a massive and collaborative scientific effort. Understanding the global climate system and its complexity requires extensive research on how the sun's energy strikes the Earth's surface, the chemistry and history of oceans and ice sheets, the compounds and motions of the atmosphere, the collective workings of the world's biomes and soils, and how human activities interact with all these systems. Over the last several decades, scientists have sounded the alarm about this global problem only after thousands of studies in many different disciplines came together to show a pattern – that the current atmospheric warming, driven by human activities, has the potential to change the planet in ways that could profoundly negatively affect human livelihood in many parts of the world.

Yet, the science was the easy part. It is devising a fair, ethical, economically viable solution to climate change that will be the true intellectual triumph of the human race, and we should be part of that triumph.

We can put "America First," only if we first put America at the international negotiating table in good faith to talk with our global community about our many options for tackling climate change. The solutions will require the ideas and creativity of all disciplines – the humanities, the social sciences, and the natural sciences – and all stakeholders – from small island nations that are already becoming uninhabitable due to rising seas to larger, richer nations whose wealth depends, for the moment, on an energy-intensive economy built on fossil fuels.

There are many reasons to be optimistic: Renewable energy is becoming cheaper, new technologies allow us to capture carbon and switch to cleaner fossil fuels and create new jobs, and new methods of agriculture allow us to save carbon in the soil and produce more food with less land, leaving more wildland to serve as a carbon sink. But, if we are not there to share our views and vision for the future, the world will, and should, proceed without us, and without regard for how new climate policies will affect our nation.

We are deluding ourselves if we think we can act alone in a world that is more intensely interconnected than ever before, particularly with regard to environmental issues. Carbon dioxide and its effects know no borders.

The only good thing that has come out of President Trump's refusal to be part of the Paris Climate Agreement is that so many entities are stepping up fill the void – states, cities, businesses, and universities, including our own, Ohio Wesleyan University, have pledged to uphold the Paris Climate Agreement, even if there is no commitment at the national level. Polls show that most U.S. citizens want to be part of the agreement, in contrast to the views of the president. Most citizens recognize the value of engaging meaningfully in a global conversation about climate change, even if they differ in how to address this problem.

As a people, we understand that we are no island.  
---  
[Laurie Anderson](https://www.owu.edu/academics/departments-programs/biological-sciences-department/faculty-staff/laurel-anderson/ "Laurie Anderson") is Professor of Botany-Microbiology, and [Nathan Rowley](https://www.owu.edu/academics/departments-programs/department-of-environment-sustainability/faculty-staff/nathan-rowley/ "Nathan Rowley") is Assistant Professor of Geology & Geography.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Climate change is a transboundary issue, necessitating global cooperation to address its impacts, such as rising sea levels, extreme weather, and biodiversity loss.
Implementing proactive measures, such as emission reductions and sustainable practices, is vital to mitigate climate impacts and ensure ecological balance for future generations.
International collaboration is essential for sharing resources, technologies, and knowledge, creating a unified response against climate change challenges that transcend national borders.
